questions.questionText: Phospholipids are classified as:
questions.explanation: Phospholipids are compound lipids because they contain fatty acids, an alcohol, and additional groups such as phosphate and often a nitrogen-containing base.

questions.questionText: Phospholipids contain fatty acids, alcohol, and:
questions.explanation: The defining feature of phospholipids is the presence of a phosphate group in addition to fatty acids and an alcohol backbone.

questions.questionText: Phospholipids are described as amphipathic because they contain:
options: A hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tails
questions.explanation: Phospholipids have a polar phosphate-containing head and nonpolar fatty acid tails. This amphipathic nature makes them ideal for biological membranes.

questions.questionText: The hydrophilic part of phospholipids usually contains:
questions.explanation: The hydrophilic head includes the phosphate group and attached alcohol or nitrogen-containing head group.

questions.questionText: The hydrophobic part of glycerophospholipids is mainly formed by:
questions.explanation: The fatty acid chains are nonpolar and hydrophobic. In membranes, they face inward away from water.

questions.questionText: The two major classes of membrane phospholipids are:
questions.explanation: Major membrane lipids include glycerophospholipids and sphingolipids. They differ mainly in their backbone.

questions.questionText: The backbone of glycerophospholipids is:
questions.explanation: Glycerophospholipids are based on a glycerol backbone with fatty acids and phosphate-containing substituents attached.

questions.questionText: The backbone of sphingolipids is:
questions.explanation: Sphingolipids use sphingosine as the backbone instead of glycerol. Sphingosine is an alcohol derived from serine.

questions.questionText: Sphingolipids are especially important in forming:
questions.explanation: Sphingolipids are major membrane lipids and are particularly important in myelin sheath structure in the central nervous system.

questions.questionText: The simplest glycerophospholipid and precursor of other members is:
questions.explanation: Phosphatidic acid is the simplest glycerophospholipid and serves as a precursor for many other phosphoglycerides.

questions.questionText: Phosphatidylcholine is also called:
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylcholine is commonly called lecithin. It is an important membrane phospholipid and also contributes to lung surfactant.

questions.questionText: Phosphatidylethanolamine is also called:
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylethanolamine is known as cephalin. It is one of the phospholipids derived from phosphatidic acid.

questions.questionText: Phosphatidylserine is formed from phosphatidic acid plus:
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylserine is formed when serine is attached as the head group to phosphatidic acid.

questions.questionText: Phosphatidylinositol is formed from phosphatidic acid plus:
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylinositol contains inositol as its head group and is important in membrane signaling pathways.

questions.questionText: Phosphatidylglycerol is formed from phosphatidic acid plus:
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylglycerol is one of the phospholipids derived from phosphatidic acid by addition of glycerol.

questions.questionText: Cardiolipin is mainly present in:
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is a special phospholipid found in the inner mitochondrial membrane and also in bacterial membranes.

questions.questionText: Cardiolipin consists of:
options: Two phosphatidic acid molecules linked through glycerol
One cholesterol ester and one bile salt
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is formed from two phosphatidic acid molecules esterified through their phosphate groups to an additional glycerol molecule.

questions.questionText: Cardiolipin is notable because it is the only human phospholipid that is:
questions.explanation: The file states that cardiolipin is the only human phospholipid that is antigenic.

questions.questionText: Plasmalogens are classified as:
questions.explanation: Plasmalogens are ether lipids. In them, an alkyl group is attached to carbon 1 of glycerol by an ether linkage rather than an ester linkage.

questions.questionText: In ether lipids, the alkyl group is attached to glycerol by:
questions.explanation: Ether lipids differ from typical glycerophospholipids because the group at carbon 1 is attached through an ether bond instead of an ester bond.

questions.questionText: Platelet activating factor is an example of:
questions.explanation: Platelet activating factor, or PAF, is an ether glycerophospholipid. It is grouped with ether lipids such as plasmalogens.

questions.questionText: Glycerophospholipids are important components of:
options: Cellular membranes, lipoproteins, bile, and lung surfactant
questions.explanation: Glycerophospholipids are major membrane lipids. They are also found in blood lipoproteins, bile, and lung surfactant.

questions.questionText: Lung surfactant helps prevent:
questions.explanation: Lung surfactant reduces surface tension and prevents alveoli from sticking together or collapsing.

questions.questionText: Phospholipids can serve as sources of polyunsaturated fatty acids, especially:
questions.explanation: Membrane phospholipids can release PUFA, especially arachidonic acid, which is a precursor for prostaglandins and other eicosanoids.

questions.questionText: Phospholipid degradation is mainly carried out by:
questions.explanation: Phospholipids are degraded by phospholipases located in cell membranes, lysosomes, and some secretions such as pancreatic juice.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why phospholipids are ideal membrane components?
options: They are amphipathic molecules with hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails
They are stored mainly as neutral fat droplets
They are used mainly as glucose precursors
questions.explanation: Phospholipids naturally form bilayers because their hydrophilic heads interact with water, while their hydrophobic fatty acid tails avoid water and face inward. This structure is the basis of biological membranes.

questions.questionText: Which part of a glycerophospholipid forms the hydrophobic region of membranes?
questions.explanation: The fatty acid chains are nonpolar and hydrophobic. In a membrane bilayer, they orient toward the interior of the membrane away from the aqueous environment.

questions.questionText: Which part of a glycerophospholipid forms the hydrophilic region?
questions.explanation: The phosphate group and its attached alcohol/head group form the polar hydrophilic part of the molecule. This part faces water on either side of the membrane.

questions.questionText: Which statement best differentiates glycerophospholipids from sphingolipids?
options: Glycerophospholipids have glycerol backbone; sphingolipids have sphingosine backbone
Glycerophospholipids have sphingosine backbone; sphingolipids have glycerol backbone
questions.explanation: The main structural difference is the backbone. Glycerophospholipids are built on glycerol, while sphingolipids are built on sphingosine.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is the simplest precursor for many glycerophospholipids?
questions.explanation: Phosphatidic acid is the basic glycerophospholipid structure. Different head groups can be added to form phosphatidylcholine, phosphatidylethanolamine, phosphatidylserine, phosphatidylinositol, and others.

questions.questionText: Phosphatidic acid contains glycerol, two fatty acids, and:
questions.explanation: Phosphatidic acid consists of a glycerol backbone with two fatty acids and a phosphate group. It is the parent structure for many glycerophospholipids.

questions.questionText: Which head group is present in phosphatidylcholine?
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylcholine, or lecithin, contains choline as the head group attached through phosphate.

questions.questionText: Which head group is present in phosphatidylethanolamine?
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylethanolamine, also called cephalin, contains ethanolamine as its head group.

questions.questionText: Which head group is present in phosphatidylserine?
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylserine is a glycerophospholipid containing serine as the attached head group.

questions.questionText: Which head group is present in phosphatidylinositol?
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylinositol contains inositol as the head group. It is especially important in cell signaling pathways.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is especially important in lung surfactant?
questions.explanation: Dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine, a type of lecithin, is a major component of lung surfactant. It reduces surface tension in alveoli and helps prevent collapse.

questions.questionText: Lung surfactant deficiency is most directly associated with:
options: Respiratory distress due to alveolar collapse
questions.explanation: Surfactant reduces alveolar surface tension. If surfactant is deficient, alveoli tend to collapse, making breathing difficult, especially in premature infants.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is mainly located in the inner mitochondrial membrane?
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is highly characteristic of the inner mitochondrial membrane and is important for mitochondrial membrane structure and function.

questions.questionText: Cardiolipin is structurally unusual because it contains:
options: Two phosphatidic acid molecules linked by glycerol
Two cholesterol rings linked by glucose
One sphingosine and one ketone body
One phosphate and no fatty acids
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is essentially diphosphatidylglycerol. It has two phosphatidic acid units linked through glycerol, making it structurally distinct.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes plasmalogens?
options: Ether phospholipids containing an ether linkage at carbon 1 of glycerol
questions.explanation: Plasmalogens differ from ordinary glycerophospholipids because the group at carbon 1 is attached by an ether linkage rather than the usual ester linkage.

questions.questionText: Plasmalogens are especially abundant in:
questions.explanation: Plasmalogens are important ether lipids found in high amounts in nervous tissue and muscle. They contribute to membrane properties.

questions.questionText: Platelet activating factor is important because it can:
options: Mediate platelet aggregation and inflammatory responses
questions.explanation: Platelet activating factor, or PAF, is a potent signaling ether lipid. It is involved in platelet activation and inflammatory reactions.

questions.questionText: Sphingomyelin contains sphingosine, fatty acid, phosphate, and:
questions.explanation: Sphingomyelin is a phosphosphingolipid. It contains a sphingosine backbone, a fatty acid, phosphate, and choline.

questions.questionText: Sphingomyelin is especially important in:
questions.explanation: Sphingomyelin is a major sphingolipid in membranes and is particularly important in the myelin sheath of nervous tissue.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains the relationship between phospholipids and eicosanoid synthesis?
options: Membrane phospholipids can release arachidonic acid for eicosanoid synthesis
Phospholipids are converted directly into glucose
Phospholipids prevent all inflammatory mediator formation
Eicosanoids are made only from cholesterol
questions.explanation: Arachidonic acid is stored mainly in membrane phospholipids. When released, it can be converted into prostaglandins, thromboxanes, and leukotrienes.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme releases arachidonic acid from membrane phospholipids?
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A2 cleaves the fatty acid at carbon 2 of glycerophospholipids. Arachidonic acid is commonly located at this position, so PLA2 releases it for eicosanoid synthesis.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase A1 cleaves the fatty acid at:
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A1 removes the fatty acid at position 1 of glycerophospholipids.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase A2 cleaves the fatty acid at:
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A2 removes the fatty acid from carbon 2. This is clinically important because arachidonic acid is often located at carbon 2.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase C cleaves phospholipids to produce:
options: Phosphatidic acid and free head group
questions.explanation: Phospholipase C cleaves before the phosphate group, producing DAG and a phosphorylated head group. In signaling, PIP2 is cleaved into DAG and IP3.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase D cleaves phospholipids to produce:
options: Phosphatidic acid and free head group
questions.explanation: Phospholipase D cleaves after the phosphate group, releasing the head group and leaving phosphatidic acid.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipase produces lysophospholipid?
questions.explanation: When phospholipase A2 removes one fatty acid, the remaining molecule is a lysophospholipid.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipase is directly involved in PIP2 signaling?
questions.explanation: Phospholipase C cleaves phosphatidylinositol bisphosphate, or PIP2, into DAG and IP3, two important second messengers.

questions.questionText: PIP2 cleavage by phospholipase C produces:
questions.explanation: PIP2 is cleaved by phospholipase C to form diacylglycerol and inositol trisphosphate. Both are important signaling molecules.

questions.questionText: DAG mainly activates:
questions.explanation: Diacylglycerol remains in the membrane and activates protein kinase C, which phosphorylates target proteins in signaling pathways.

questions.questionText: IP3 mainly causes:
questions.explanation: IP3 diffuses through the cytosol and stimulates calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum. This calcium acts as a second messenger.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is the precursor of PIP2?
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylinositol can be phosphorylated to phosphatidylinositol phosphates, including PIP2, which participates in signal transduction.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why phosphatidylinositol is important despite being a membrane lipid?
options: It can generate second messengers after phospholipase C activation
It is the main storage form of fatty acids
It is converted directly into ketone bodies
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylinositol derivatives are involved in signaling. PIP2 cleavage produces DAG and IP3, linking membrane phospholipids to intracellular responses.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme degrades sphingomyelin?
questions.explanation: Sphingomyelinase hydrolyzes sphingomyelin into ceramide and phosphorylcholine.

questions.questionText: Sphingomyelin degradation produces:
questions.explanation: Sphingomyelinase breaks sphingomyelin to form ceramide and phosphorylcholine. Ceramide can have structural and signaling roles.

questions.questionText: Which statement best differentiates glycerophospholipid degradation from sphingomyelin degradation?
options: Glycerophospholipids are degraded by phospholipases; sphingomyelin is degraded by sphingomyelinase
Both are degraded only by hexokinase
Sphingomyelin is degraded by glycogen phosphorylase
questions.explanation: Different phospholipid classes require different enzymes. Glycerophospholipids are cleaved by phospholipases A1, A2, C, and D, while sphingomyelin is cleaved by sphingomyelinase.

questions.questionText: Which statement best summarizes phospholipase A2 action?
options: It removes the fatty acid at C2 and may release arachidonic acid
It cleaves PIP2 into DAG and IP3
It releases the head group and leaves phosphatidic acid
questions.explanation: PLA2 is high-yield because it releases arachidonic acid from membrane phospholipids. Arachidonic acid is then used for eicosanoid synthesis.

questions.questionText: Which statement best summarizes phospholipase C action?
options: It cleaves PIP2 into DAG and IP3
It removes fatty acid from carbon 2
It releases phosphatidic acid and free head group
questions.explanation: Phospholipase C is central in signal transduction. DAG activates PKC, while IP3 increases intracellular calcium.

questions.questionText: Which statement best summarizes phospholipase D action?
options: It produces phosphatidic acid and free head group
It releases arachidonic acid from C2
questions.explanation: Phospholipase D cleaves the bond between phosphate and the head group, leaving phosphatidic acid.

questions.questionText: A signal activates phospholipase C. Which intracellular changes are expected?
options: Increased DAG, IP3, PKC activation, and calcium release
questions.explanation: PLC activation cleaves PIP2 into DAG and IP3. DAG activates PKC, and IP3 releases calcium from intracellular stores.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is most directly linked to intracellular calcium signaling?
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylinositol derivatives such as PIP2 are cleaved to form IP3, which releases calcium from the endoplasmic reticulum.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is most directly linked to mitochondrial membrane function?
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is highly enriched in the inner mitochondrial membrane. It helps maintain mitochondrial membrane structure and supports respiratory chain function.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is most directly linked to alveolar stability?
questions.explanation: Dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine is a major lung surfactant phospholipid. It reduces surface tension and helps prevent alveolar collapse.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is most directly linked to platelet activation?
questions.explanation: Platelet activating factor is an ether lipid that participates in platelet aggregation and inflammatory signaling.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid class is most associated with myelin?
questions.explanation: Sphingolipids, especially sphingomyelin and related lipids, are important structural components of myelin sheaths.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement best summarizes phospholipid metabolism?
options: Phospholipids are amphipathic membrane lipids that support membrane structure, signaling, surfactant function, mitochondrial membranes, myelin, and arachidonic acid release
Phospholipids are mainly stored as neutral fat droplets and used only for ATP storage
Phospholipids are ketone bodies produced by liver mitochondria
Phospholipids are only dietary digestion products with no signaling role
questions.explanation: This summarizes the topic well. Phospholipids are not only structural membrane components; they also participate in signaling, inflammation, surfactant action, mitochondrial function, and nervous tissue structure.

questions.questionText: A premature infant develops respiratory distress due to alveolar collapse. Deficiency of which phospholipid-related substance best explains this?
questions.explanation: Dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine is a major component of lung surfactant. Surfactant reduces alveolar surface tension, so deficiency increases the tendency of alveoli to collapse, causing respiratory distress.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is most directly related to inner mitochondrial membrane structure and function?
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is characteristically found in the inner mitochondrial membrane. It supports mitochondrial membrane organization and is important for normal respiratory chain function.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why cardiolipin is structurally unique?
options: It contains two phosphatidic acid units linked through glycerol
It contains sphingosine instead of glycerol
It is made only of cholesterol and glucose
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is also called diphosphatidylglycerol. It is formed from two phosphatidic acid molecules joined through a glycerol bridge, making it different from ordinary glycerophospholipids.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is antigenic and historically important in some serologic tests?
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin is noted as the only human phospholipid that is antigenic. This makes it clinically recognizable in immunologic/serologic contexts.

questions.questionText: A membrane signaling pathway activates phospholipase C. Which products are formed from PIP2?
questions.explanation: Phospholipase C cleaves PIP2 into two second messengers: DAG and IP3. DAG remains in the membrane and activates protein kinase C, while IP3 releases calcium from intracellular stores.

questions.questionText: Which second messenger remains in the membrane and activates protein kinase C?
questions.explanation: Diacylglycerol is lipid-soluble, so it remains in the membrane. It activates protein kinase C, which phosphorylates target proteins.

questions.questionText: Which second messenger diffuses through the cytosol and promotes calcium release?
questions.explanation: IP3 is water-soluble and diffuses through the cytosol. It binds receptors on the endoplasmic reticulum and triggers release of Ca²⁺ into the cytoplasm.

questions.questionText: Arachidonic acid is released from membrane phospholipids mainly by which enzyme?
questions.explanation: Arachidonic acid is commonly esterified at carbon 2 of membrane phospholipids. Phospholipase A2 cleaves this position, releasing arachidonic acid for eicosanoid synthesis.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipase action best matches production of phosphatidic acid?
options: Phospholipase D removes the head group
Phospholipase A2 removes the C2 fatty acid
Phospholipase C removes the C1 fatty acid
questions.explanation: Phospholipase D cleaves between phosphate and the head group. The remaining lipid is phosphatidic acid.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipase action best matches production of lysophospholipid?
options: Phospholipase A2 removes one fatty acid
Phospholipase C cleaves PIP2 into DAG and IP3
Phospholipase D removes the head group
questions.explanation: A lysophospholipid is formed when one fatty acid is removed from a phospholipid. PLA2 removes the fatty acid at carbon 2, leaving a lysophospholipid.

questions.questionText: A drug inhibits phospholipase A2. Which downstream product would most directly decrease?
questions.explanation: PLA2 releases arachidonic acid from membrane phospholipids. If PLA2 is inhibited, less arachidonic acid is available for prostaglandin, thromboxane, and leukotriene synthesis.

questions.questionText: Which statement best differentiates phospholipase C and phospholipase D?
options: PLC produces DAG and phosphorylated head group; PLD produces phosphatidic acid and free head group
PLC removes C2 fatty acid; PLD removes C1 fatty acid
PLC degrades sphingomyelin; PLD forms ceramide
Both produce exactly the same products
questions.explanation: PLC cleaves before the phosphate group, generating DAG plus a phosphorylated head group such as IP3. PLD cleaves after the phosphate group, leaving phosphatidic acid and releasing the head group.

questions.questionText: Which phospholipid is most directly involved in generating DAG and IP3?
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylinositol can be phosphorylated to PIP2. PIP2 is the substrate for phospholipase C, producing DAG and IP3.

questions.questionText: A defect in sphingomyelinase would most directly impair formation of:
questions.explanation: Sphingomyelinase hydrolyzes sphingomyelin into ceramide and phosphorylcholine. This is the main degradation route for sphingomyelin.

questions.questionText: Which molecule is produced from sphingomyelin degradation and can also act in signaling?
questions.explanation: Ceramide is produced by sphingomyelinase action on sphingomyelin. It is not only a structural sphingolipid intermediate but also can act as a signaling molecule.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why sphingomyelin is especially important in nervous tissue?
options: It is a major lipid component of myelin sheath
It is the main storage form of glucose
It is the main ketone body in brain
It is the major enzyme of β-oxidation
questions.explanation: Sphingomyelin and related sphingolipids are enriched in myelin. Myelin requires lipid-rich membranes to insulate axons and support nerve conduction.

questions.questionText: Which statement best differentiates ordinary glycerophospholipids from plasmalogens?
options: Plasmalogens have an ether linkage at C1 instead of the usual ester linkage
Ordinary glycerophospholipids contain sphingosine instead of glycerol
questions.explanation: Plasmalogens are ether glycerophospholipids. The C1 position contains an ether-linked group rather than the usual ester-linked fatty acid.

questions.questionText: Which lipid is best matched with its function?
options: Platelet activating factor — platelet aggregation and inflammatory signaling
questions.explanation: PAF is an ether lipid involved in platelet activation and inflammatory responses. Cardiolipin is mitochondrial, while lecithin is important in membranes and surfactant.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement best summarizes phospholipase actions?
options: PLA1 removes C1 fatty acid, PLA2 removes C2 fatty acid, PLC forms DAG plus phosphorylated head group, and PLD forms phosphatidic acid plus free head group
PLA1 forms DAG/IP3, PLA2 forms phosphatidic acid, PLC releases arachidonic acid, and PLD forms ceramide
Phospholipases only synthesize phospholipids and never degrade them
questions.explanation: This is the main enzyme map for glycerophospholipid degradation. Each phospholipase cleaves a specific bond and produces characteristic products.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement best summarizes phospholipid metabolism clinically and biochemically?
options: Phospholipids form membranes and specialized structures, participate in signaling, release arachidonic acid, support surfactant and mitochondrial function, and are degraded by specific phospholipases or sphingomyelinase
Phospholipids are only stored energy molecules and have no signaling role
Phospholipids are made only in liver mitochondria to produce ketone bodies
Phospholipids are water-soluble molecules transported freely like glucose
questions.explanation: This is the full high-yield picture. Phospholipids are structural membrane lipids, but they also have specialized roles in lung surfactant, mitochondria, myelin, inflammation, platelet activation, and signal transduction.
